speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're talking about one of the most practical parts of working with Git and GitHub: keeping secrets out of your repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And by secrets, we mean things like API keys, passwords, tokens, private keys, database URLs, all the stuff that absolutely should not end up in a commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This lesson is for anyone who commits code or documentation. You do not need to be a security expert. In fact, most contributor security problems are not dramatic hacks. They're accidents. A token pasted into a config file. A.env file committed by mistake. A credential file that slipped into staging.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the reassuring part is that this is mostly about habits.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A few good habits prevent most of these problems.

speaker: ALEX
text: A quick accessibility note before we dive in. This reference is structured from understanding the risk, to prevention, to recovery, to daily habits. If you're using a screen reader, the code blocks include exact terminal commands and gitignore patterns, so it can help to switch to a focused reading mode before copying. If you have low vision, the most important warnings in the original reference are emphasized, and the checklist at the end is designed to be used as a repeatable routine.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is both a lesson and a reference workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You can listen straight through, or come back to the prevention steps, the recovery steps, and the checklist later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with why this matters. If a secret is committed to a public GitHub repository, even for a few seconds, you should treat it as compromised.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Even if you notice immediately and delete it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Even then. There are continuous bots scanning public repositories for secrets. They can harvest exposed credentials within seconds. Also, deleting the file from the latest version of the project does not remove it from Git history. And if the repository has already been forked, that history may exist elsewhere too. Search engines may also have indexed content before you removed it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the phrase to remember is: deleting the visible file is not the same as removing the leak.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly said. And the consequences can be expensive or damaging very quickly. A cloud key can rack up charges. A GitHub personal access token could allow access to repositories or let someone act as you. A payment platform key could be abused for fraudulent activity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Is there any good news here?

speaker: ALEX
text: There is. GitHub can automatically revoke some of its own tokens when it detects them in a commit, like certain GitHub personal access tokens or app tokens. But for many third-party services, like AWS or Stripe, you must rotate the secret yourself, and fast.

speaker: ALEX
text: The first major prevention tool is the.gitignore file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the file that tells Git what not to track.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If a file is listed in.gitignore and Git is not already tracking it, that file won't show up in git status, won't be staged by normal add commands, and won't be committed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What kinds of things belong there?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three big categories. First, secrets and credentials. That includes environment files like.env,.env.local, environment-specific local files, generic star-dot-env patterns, key files like.pem,.key,.p12,.pfx, and SSH private key names like id_rsa or id_ed25519. Also credential files such as credentials.json, secrets.json, config slash secrets.yml, or cloud credential files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a file stores a secret, it is a strong candidate for.gitignore.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Second category: editor and operating system clutter. On macOS that might be.DS_Store or.AppleDouble. On Windows, Thumbs.db or desktop.ini. For editors, JetBrains projects often ignore.idea slash. VS Code is nuanced: some teams commit selected settings, and some do not. So.vscode settings may be optional depending on team practice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That nuance matters, because learners sometimes assume every editor-generated file should always be ignored.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Some workspace settings are intentionally shared. Third category: build output and dependencies. For Node projects that often means node_modules, dist, and build. For Python it might be __pycache__,.pyc files,.venv, or venv. And then general clutter like logs, temp files, and caches.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But.gitignore has an important limitation, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: It does..gitignore only stops untracked files from being added. If Git is already tracking a file, adding it to.gitignore does not magically untrack it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So how do you check?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can ask Git directly whether a file is tracked. For example, to check.env, run git ls-files space.env. If Git returns that filename, it's already tracked.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then you need to untrack it without deleting your local copy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The usual command is git rm --cached space.env. That removes it from Git's tracking index but leaves the file on your machine. Then add.env to.gitignore and commit that change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's such an important distinction: remove from Git tracking, not from your disk.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes.

speaker: ALEX
text: You can also create a global gitignore file for patterns you never want in any repository on your computer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Like OS junk files or personal editor clutter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. One approach is to create a file such as tilde slash.gitignore_global, then tell Git to use it with a global config setting: core.excludesfile pointed at that file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So project-specific ignores stay in the repository's.gitignore, and machine-specific noise can live in the global ignore.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the clean mental model.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone doesn't want to write all these patterns from scratch?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub maintains.gitignore templates for many languages and frameworks. You can choose one when creating a repository on GitHub, or download one for an existing project, such as the Node template, and append it to your.gitignore.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's talk about the right way to store secrets: environment variables.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning the secret lives outside your repository, and your code reads it in when it runs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The anti-pattern is hardcoding a secret directly into source code. For example, writing an API key as a literal string in Python or JavaScript. The safer pattern is to read that value from the environment instead, using something like os.environ.get in Python or process.env in JavaScript.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the code can be committed, but the secret value stays outside the repo.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Locally, many projects use a.env file to store those environment variables. That's convenient and common, but that file must never be committed. It belongs in.gitignore.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the values inside might include a GitHub token, a database URL, or a payment secret key.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The code that loads the.env file can be in the repository. The actual.env file stays on your machine.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should teams share secrets, then, if not by email or chat?

speaker: ALEX
text: Never through Slack, email, or GitHub comments. Safer options include GitHub Actions Secrets for automation, a password manager with secure sharing for team credentials, or a dedicated secrets manager for production systems.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give the GitHub Actions version in plain language?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. In a GitHub Actions workflow, you define an environment variable from a stored GitHub secret, like secrets dot API_KEY, and then the workflow can use it during deployment without placing the actual secret in the workflow file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the workflow references the secret by name, but the value lives in repository or organization settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next habit: review before you commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This sounds simple, but it's probably the most powerful routine in the whole lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: I think so too. Before committing, inspect exactly what you've staged. The key command is git diff --staged. That shows the content that is actually going into the commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And you can also review a specific staged file the same way.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Use git diff --staged followed by a path if you want a narrower review. As you scan the diff, look for hardcoded passwords, tokens, API keys,.env files, credential files, or even TODO comments that mention sensitive details.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last one is sneaky. Sometimes the leak is not the credential itself, but a note about where the credential lives.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly right.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is where the warning against git add dot comes in.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. git add. stages everything it sees in the working tree. That is fast, but risky. A safer habit is to stage only known-clean files, like git add followed by specific paths. Or use git add -p, patch mode, which lets you review changes hunk by hunk and decide what to stage.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Patch mode can feel slower at first, but it's a wonderful guardrail.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And after staging, check git status to see what's staged and what isn't, then use git diff --staged for the actual content review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also mentions an AI-assisted review step.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. After staging changes, you can use a tool like GitHub Copilot Chat and ask it to review your staged diff for accidentally included secrets, API keys, or credentials. That is not a replacement for your own review, but it can be an extra check.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's add automation with pre-commit hooks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: A pre-commit hook is a script that runs automatically when you try to commit, and it can block the commit if it finds a problem.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Think of it as a safety net. The appendix presents three options.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the practical overview.

speaker: ALEX
text: Option A is detect-secrets, recommended in the source and Python-based. The workflow is: install the tool, create a baseline scan of your existing code, review that baseline so known non-secrets are marked safe, install the pre-commit hook, and test it manually.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why the baseline step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because many repositories already contain strings that look secret-like but are harmless examples, test values, or false positives. The baseline gives the tool a starting point so it can focus on new problems.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So baseline first, then hook, then test.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about Option B?

speaker: ALEX
text: Option B is gitleaks. It's Go-based and known for being easy to run with minimal dependencies. The source calls out installation on macOS and Windows, then two useful scan modes: scanning the entire repository history for secrets, and scanning only staged changes, meaning what you're about to commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That distinction matters. One mode is for a deep audit. The other is for day-to-day protection.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The source also describes adding gitleaks as a manual pre-commit hook by creating a script in.git slash hooks slash pre-commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So that's a more hands-on setup.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Option C?

speaker: ALEX
text: Option C is the pre-commit framework, which manages multiple hooks for you. The workflow is: install pre-commit, create a.pre-commit-config.yaml file in the repository root, define the hooks you want there, install the hooks, and optionally run them manually against all files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of wiring each hook by hand, you use a framework to manage them consistently.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. For contributors working across multiple repos, that can be especially nice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If a learner is choosing, what's the simplest advice?

speaker: ALEX
text: Pick one automated secret scanning approach and actually use it. The perfect tool is less important than having a hook that runs before secrets leave your machine.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, let's tackle the stressful scenario. I accidentally committed a secret. What now?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step one: rotate the secret immediately.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before anything else.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before anything else. Assume the old secret is compromised. Generate a new token, key, or password with the provider, and revoke the old one if possible.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Step two is asking whether it was pushed publicly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If the secret was committed locally but not pushed yet, you're in a much better position. One safe pattern is to undo the last commit while keeping your changes staged, remove the secret from the file, then re-add and recommit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So you fix the content before it ever reaches a public remote.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Edit the file, remove the secret, stage the cleaned version, and create a new commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But if it was pushed, deleting the line in a new commit is not enough because the secret still exists in history.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's step three: remove the secret from Git history.

speaker: ALEX
text: The recommended modern tool in the appendix is git-filter-repo. You install git-filter-repo, then either remove a specific file from all history or replace a specific secret string throughout all history.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if the problem is, say, an accidentally committed.env file, you can strip that file from every historical commit. And if the problem is a literal token pasted in several places, you can replace that string across history.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's an alternative.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, BFG Repo-Cleaner, which is fast and Java-based. The appendix notes downloading it from the BFG project site. With BFG, you can remove a file from all history or replace secret strings. For string replacement, the source calls out creating a passwords.txt file with one secret per line.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So both tools are about rewriting history to eliminate the old secret traces.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then comes the part people are often nervous about: force push.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Step four is force pushing the cleaned history to the remote. Because you've rewritten commit history, a normal push won't work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that can affect collaborators.

speaker: ALEX
text: It can. Rewriting history is disruptive, which is another reason prevention matters so much. After that, step five is to tell GitHub to rescan, so its secret scanning systems can reassess the repository state.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we summarize the decision flow in plain language?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. First: rotate the secret immediately. Second: if it was never pushed, clean it locally and recommit. Third: if it was pushed, remove it from history, then force push the cleaned history, and follow up with GitHub rescanning and any provider-specific cleanup.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now, GitHub also has built-in push protection.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the feature that can stop you from pushing certain detected secrets.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Push protection checks for supported secret patterns and can block the push before the secret lands in the remote repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds great, but I want learners to hear this clearly: it's helpful, not magical.

speaker: ALEX
text: Precisely. Push protection covers many common secret types, but not every possible secret. You still need.gitignore, environment variables, review habits, and ideally hooks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if push protection blocks you?

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat that as a serious warning. Stop, inspect the file or diff, and determine whether the detected string is a real secret. If it is, remove it and store it properly. If it's a false positive, follow your project's and GitHub's guidance carefully before bypassing anything.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And can you check whether a repository has push protection enabled?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix includes checking your repository's push protection status through GitHub's security settings and secret scanning features. For learners, the main concept is: know whether your repo has that guardrail, but don't rely on it as the only one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's talk about secure credential storage more broadly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning not just secrets in code, but where your Git and development tools store credentials.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The big rule is never store credentials in plaintext. Not in a plain text file, not hardcoded in a script, and not casually embedded in git config in a way that exposes the secret.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So where should they go instead?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use your operating system's secure credential store when possible. On macOS, that's Keychain. On Windows, Git for Windows typically integrates with Credential Manager. On Linux, a common approach is the libsecret store, though it may require installation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And a password manager?

speaker: ALEX
text: Also a good option, especially for personal organization and for teams that need secure sharing. The appendix specifically points learners toward using a password manager rather than leaving tokens in notes or loose files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you check what Git is currently using?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can inspect which credential helper is set in Git configuration. The practical point is to verify that Git is using a secure helper instead of relying on insecure plaintext storage.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's turn all of this into a repeatable checklist.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Yes, because daily routines are what actually keep repos safe.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before committing: make sure secrets are in environment variables, not code. Confirm.env and credential files are ignored. Stage carefully, not blindly. Review staged changes with git diff --staged. If you use patch mode or a pre-commit hook, this is where they help most.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before pushing: double-check what is about to leave your machine, make sure no secret warnings are appearing, and pay attention if push protection or a hook blocks you.

speaker: ALEX
text: Repository setup, one time: create a strong.gitignore, consider a global gitignore for OS and editor noise, set up a pre-commit secret scanner, and use secure credential helpers on your machine.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you're a maintainer?

speaker: ALEX
text: Encourage or require these habits project-wide. Provide a good.gitignore. Document how contributors should handle.env files and local configuration. Consider pre-commit tooling, secret scanning, and push protection. Make the safe path the easy path.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap up, I want to return to the teaching structure for accessibility. This appendix was designed so learners can approach it in multiple ways: start with why leaks matter, jump to prevention if that's your immediate need, or revisit the checklist before every push.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And the authoritative guidance behind this material comes from GitHub's code security and secret scanning documentation, along with related GitHub security feature references and changelog updates.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this isn't just folklore. It's grounded in the platform's actual security guidance.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give us one final compact version?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. Don't commit secrets. Ignore the right files. Store secrets in environment variables. Review staged changes before every commit. Use automated detection with hooks. If a secret leaks, rotate it immediately and clean history if needed. Use GitHub push protection when available, but don't depend on it alone. And store credentials in secure system stores, not plaintext.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's the habit loop.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And it's one of the best ways to protect both your own work and every project you contribute to.
